subject: Chemistry
subtitle: ATOMS & TRENDS — PERIODIC TABLE · BONDING · ATOMIC STRUCTURE · HISTORY
theory_html:
CHEMISTRY — ATOMS & TRENDS

PERIODIC TABLE · BONDING · ATOMIC STRUCTURE · HISTORY

History of Chemistry

~450 BC
ডেমোক্রিটাস / Democritus
Proposed matter is made of indivisible "atomos." Pure philosophy with no experiments. Aristotle rejected this in favour of four elements (earth, water, fire, air), delaying atomic theory by over 2,000 years.

8th-13th c
জাবির ইবনে হাইয়ান / Jabir ibn Hayyan
Father of Chemistry. Arab chemist who pioneered experimental methods, distillation, crystallisation, and described many acids (sulfuric, nitric, hydrochloric). His work preserved and extended Greek knowledge through the Dark Ages.

1661
রবার্ট বয়েল / Robert Boyle
The Sceptical Chymist: separated chemistry from alchemy, defined elements as irreducible substances that cannot be broken down further. Founded scientific chemistry. Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ at constant temperature.

1789
ল্যাভয়সিয়ে / Lavoisier
Proved conservation of mass ("matter is neither created nor destroyed"), named oxygen and hydrogen, wrote the first modern chemistry textbook. Called the "Father of Modern Chemistry." Ironically guillotined during the French Revolution in 1794.

1803
জন ডাল্টন / John Dalton
Modern Atomic Theory: elements made of identical, indivisible atoms; different elements have different atomic weights; compounds form by fixed whole-number ratios of atoms; chemical reactions rearrange (not create/destroy) atoms.

1869
দিমিত্রি মেন্ডেলিভ / Dmitri Mendeleev
Arranged 63 known elements by atomic weight and recurring properties. Left deliberate gaps for undiscovered elements, predicting properties of gallium (eka-aluminium), scandium (eka-boron), and germanium (eka-silicon) — all confirmed within 15 years. Revolutionary act of scientific faith.

1897
J.J. টমসন / J.J. Thomson
Discovered the electron via cathode ray tube experiments. First subatomic particle found, disproving the solid atom model. Proposed the "plum pudding" model: electrons embedded in a diffuse positive sphere.

1911
আর্নেস্ট রাদারফোর্ড / Ernest Rutherford
Gold foil experiment: fired alpha particles at gold foil. Most passed through, but 1 in 8000 bounced back ("like firing artillery shells at tissue paper and they bounced back"). Conclusion: atom is mostly empty space with a tiny, dense, positively-charged nucleus.

1913
নীলস বোর / Niels Bohr
Quantum atomic model: electrons occupy discrete energy levels (shells n=1,2,3...). Electrons emit or absorb photons when jumping between levels (E = hf). Explained hydrogen's emission spectrum exactly. Introduced the concept of quantised angular momentum.

1926
শ্রোডিঙ্গার / Schrödinger
Quantum mechanical model: electrons exist as probability clouds (orbitals: s, p, d, f), not fixed circular paths. Wave equation describes the probability of finding an electron at any location. This is the model still used today.

Periodic Trends — In Depth

পারমাণবিক ব্যাসার্ধ / Atomic Radius:
Decreases left→right across a period: increasing nuclear charge (more protons) pulls electron clouds closer without adding new shells. Increases top→bottom down a group: each period adds a new electron shell, increasing atomic size. Li (152 pm) → F (64 pm) across period 2. Cs is the largest non-radioactive atom.

আয়নিকরণ শক্তি / Ionization Energy (IE₁):
Energy to remove the outermost electron. Increases left→right (stronger nuclear attraction, same shielding). Decreases top→bottom (more shielding from inner electrons, greater atomic radius). Anomalies: IE₁(O) < IE₁(N) due to electron pairing in 2p. IE₁(Al) < IE₁(Mg) due to 3p vs 3s subshell. Highest: He (2372 kJ/mol). Lowest: Cs (376 kJ/mol).

তড়িৎঋণাত্মকতা / Electronegativity:
Ability to attract electrons in a bond. Increases toward top-right of the periodic table. F = 3.98 (highest, Pauling scale). Fr ≈ 0.7 (lowest). If ΔEN > 1.7: ionic bond. 0.4–1.7: polar covalent. < 0.4: non-polar covalent. Determines molecular polarity, solubility, and reactivity.

ইলেকট্রন আসক্তি / Electron Affinity:
Energy change when a gaseous atom gains one electron. Generally increases left→right. Cl has the highest electron affinity of all elements (−349 kJ/mol), higher than F due to smaller atomic size causing more electron-electron repulsion in F. Halogens and chalcogens have high electron affinities. Noble gases: near zero (full shells).

ধাতব বৈশিষ্ট্য / Metallic Character:
Increases bottom-left: easily lose electrons. Decreases top-right: metals → metalloids → non-metals → noble gases. Fr is the most metallic. F is the most non-metallic. Metalloids (Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te) act as semiconductors.

Atomic Structure

Z = atomic number (প্রোটন সংখ্যা)
A = Z + N (ভর সংখ্যা)
Electron config: 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶...
Subshell capacities: s(2), p(6), d(10), f(14). Aufbau principle: fill lowest energy orbitals first. Pauli exclusion: max 2 electrons per orbital (opposite spins). Hund's rule: one electron per degenerate orbital before pairing (maximise unpaired spins).

If nucleus were a marble (1 cm), the atom would be a football stadium (100 m). Atom is 99.9999999% empty space. Proton mass ≈ 1836 × electron mass. Nuclear density ≈ 10¹⁷ kg/m³ (a teaspoon of nuclear matter weighs ~10⁸ tonnes).

Chemical Bonding

আয়নিক বন্ধন / Ionic Bond: Complete electron transfer from metal to non-metal. Results in oppositely charged ions held by electrostatic attraction. High melting/boiling points. Conducts electricity when dissolved in water or molten. Examples: NaCl, MgO, CaCl₂.

সহযোজী বন্ধন / Covalent Bond: Electron sharing between non-metals. Single (σ), double (σ+π), triple (σ+2π). Polar if ΔEN > 0.4 (H₂O, HCl). Non-polar if ΔEN ≈ 0 (Cl₂, O₂). VSEPR theory determines 3D molecular shape. Electronegativity differences govern polarity.

ধাতব বন্ধন / Metallic Bond: Delocalised electron "sea" surrounding positive metal cations. Explains malleability, ductility, conductivity, and metallic lustre. Strength varies: Na (weak, soft) → W (very strong, highest mp = 3422°C).

আন্তর-আণবিক শক্তি / IMFs: London dispersion (all molecules, weakest) < Dipole-dipole < Hydrogen bonds (N-H, O-H, F-H — strongest, explains water's anomalous properties). H-bonds give DNA its shape and allow self-replication.

pH, Acids & Bases

pH = −log[H⁺]
pOH = −log[OH⁻]
pH + pOH = 14 (at 25°C)
Kw = [H⁺][OH⁻] = 10⁻¹⁴
pH < 7: acidic · pH = 7: neutral · pH > 7: basic. Each unit = 10× concentration change. Common pH values: stomach acid (1.5), lemon (2.3), black coffee (5.0), pure water (7.0), blood (7.4), seawater (8.1), baking soda (8.3), bleach (12.5).

Three Acid-Base Theories: Arrhenius (H⁺/OH⁻ in water only) → Brønsted-Lowry (proton donor/acceptor, broader — works in any solvent) → Lewis (electron pair acceptor/donor, broadest — explains reactions with no H⁺ at all). Each theory is a superset of the previous.

Gas Laws & Stoichiometry

PV = nRT (Ideal Gas)
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ (Boyle — const. T)
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂ (Charles — const. P)
P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂ (Gay-Lussac — const. V)
n = m/M (moles)
R = 8.314 J/(mol·K). Avogadro's number: 6.022×10²³ particles/mol. 1 mol ideal gas at STP (0°C, 1 atm) = 22.4 L. Stoichiometry: balance atoms on both sides. Limiting reagent determines product yield. % yield = (actual ÷ theoretical) × 100.
